item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Posting here for clarity, as this discussion dragged the post down too long.

The image was indeed taken from the Avengers TV show which is under All Rights Reserved, and thus is not compliant with the wiki's CC 3.0 license. It has since been removed.

item.title: Blackboxing Error
item.content:encoded: Your first mention of Samuel L. Jackson has one too many blackboxes (8) in the last name, but the next mentions have only 7 boxes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, but minor gripe. The word "inanimate" is used a few times to describe the dolls, but the recordings still show them moving around. I know the context is "normally inanimate" but it seems a little janky to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it requires extensive containment procedures for it to be 'contained', which it technically isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
I love this, but why is it rated keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could upvote a comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If SCP-2034 became public knowledge, I find it hard to believe that it alone would be capable of causing society to collapse

If photos can randomly change, how can we trust legal or historical documents to stay the same? How about our lawbooks? How many oeopke were convicted or given unduly harsh sentences? What happens if your SSN suddenly changes, or your birth certificate suddenly claims you're a Zimbabwean? How can we trust scientific journals?

Sure, this only affects photographs, but when people start to think that one form of documentation can randomly change, they may start to distrust all forms of documentation. And then everything goes to hell as people start doubting the laws written by their governments, that their money is worth anything, that their own holy book may actually be telling new and different falsehoods on a week-to-week basis.

You can't just look at how a SCP will directly impact society, but how it will indirectly impact it as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I admit that this thing would cause problems if the public knew about it and is a pain in the ass for the Foundation to contain, I don't really understand why this is Keter. If SCP-2034 became public knowledge, I find it hard to believe that it alone would be capable of causing society to collapse, or bring about the end of the world. It may just be me, but this seems more like a Euclid and nothing like a Keter.

Other than that, I think that this was very well done. It was an interesting concept with decent execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is the shit i love. quiet, unnerving phenomena. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit was reverted for correcting what wasn't a spelling error to begin with. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea how I missed your question for so long.

Yes. Yes, you should take it down; Bertelsmann may not be amused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because of concept and good execution, though this is one of those SCPs, like SCP-2137, where I question the Foundation's ability to realistically contain public knowledge of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
prolly nothing iunno...
– hide block

Random people who appear on video tapes or photographs are suddenly showing up in those videos/photos as ventriloquist dummy-versions of themselves. Nothing happens to the actual person, and the length of time that they appear as a dummy is completely at random. It affects every piece of video or photograph the person has been in at any point in their lives. For every person affected (except Tobritza Pemberton) this effect suddenly ceases after a certain period of time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um, Am I missing something? What is happening here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very creepy. Just one thing though:
"All photo/video footage of the actor depicted a non-anomalous ███████ █. ████████"
This breaks the flow a bit, and the use of "a" implies that there are multiple non-anomalous ███████ █. ████████.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice. I enjoy how this showcases that the definition of "Keter" is about containability rather than any danger involved, I find the idea behind the article compelling, and not the least, experiencing "Tussaud syndrome" would creep the hell out of me.
Enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't know The Wire, but I appreciate the change. :) Upboat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I removed Brad Pitt in favor of another "The Wire" refrence

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Brad Pitt reference really threw me off, as well. I would have expected the name to be black boxed in an SCP article. It would also make the article more timeless, as you can put any actors name in there (even when Brad Pitt is no longer in the game). The reader can use their imagination to pick an actor that fits those qualities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Extremely neat concept. I think you should touch on how many people seem to be susceptible to this, though. Might add to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm definitely on board with this idea, but third the Pitt mention breaking suspension of disbelief. Consider redacting it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: should I remove that as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crediting an image to a TV screenshot is a) very useful in terms of allowing provenance to be tracked, and b) completely useless in terms of exempting it from copyright. It's from The Avengers? Then it's property of Bertelsmann. And much as we don't want to attract the wrath of Getty Images, we even less want to attract the attention of Bertelsmann.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Confirmed. I'll take it down for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm on mobile right now, so I can't use google image reverse search to verify the source. I do know that all images from Getty Images require payment for usage as a stock photo site, and that they have a tendency to file random lawsuits. Even if its images may be floating everywhere around the web, we really can't afford to get sued.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that's not how copyright works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure about the Brad Pitt part. I feel like you could redact the name and keep the dates in and it would be more effective. As it stands, it comes off like this was meant to poke fun at him for reasons I'm not aware of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wow, I love how the first image sometimes changes.
this is a really great concept and is very subtly creepy. At first I didn't agree with the Keter class since it is not actually dangerous but after I thought about it a little more it makes sense.
Interesting mention of Brad Pitt. Is that some kind of reference to or joke about something in Brad Pitt's history that I don't know about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no it is not

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have an incident "XX-1"

Is it supposed to be like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
first picture is of British actor Claude Dampier, photo credit to Edward Gooch of Getty Images.

Second picture is from The Avengers TV show, episode "How to Succeed at Murder"
